chunk_summary: Stunting in chronic diseases is driven by inflammation, nutrient losses, and energy imbalances. Management requires disease-specific interventions, multidisciplinary care, and realistic functional goals.
learning_objectives: Understand the mechanisms of stunting in chronic organ diseases and immunocompromised states.
Identify disease-specific nutritional interventions for conditions like CKD, liver disease, and congenital heart disease.
Recognize the importance of multidisciplinary care in managing stunting in chronic diseases.
Learn to document functional outcomes alongside growth metrics in chronic disease management.
key_facts: In congenital heart disease, feeding fatigue and tachypnea increase energy needs; smaller, frequent feeds with higher energy density are recommended.
Chronic kidney disease (CKD) stunting is multifactorial; KDIGO 2024 guidelines recommend correcting acidosis, treating anemia, and tailoring protein/phosphate intake.
Chronic liver disease causes fat malabsorption and fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies; targeted supplementation is essential.
In HIV and TB, poor growth should not be solely attributed to diet; aggressive infection treatment and adherence support are critical.
Functional outcomes like school attendance and reduced infection days should be documented alongside growth velocity.
reference_quote: KDIGO 2024 CKD Guideline key takeaways for pediatricians recommend early nephrology referral when hypertension, polyuria, or persistent biochemical abnormalities coexist with poor growth.
document_key_topics: Functional outcomes in chronic disease care
